  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sanskrit (code=sa, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Kanpur Nagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Sanskrit.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Kanpur Nagar
- first_language: Sanskrit
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, with a matriculation education, currently not employed. He is unmarried and lives in an urban setting, speaking Sanskrit as his first language with functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes anxiety‑driven yoga, academic stress, and a preference for street food.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sanskrit (code=sa, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Kanpur Nagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Sanskrit.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Kanpur Nagar
- first_language: Sanskrit
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, with a matriculation education, currently not employed. He resides in an urban setting and speaks Sanskrit as his first language, with functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes occasional anxiety‑driven yoga, a preference for street food, and a cultural background that blends Islamic traditions with an appreciation for classical Indian arts.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC ...]] Village [[VILLAGE|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
प्राथमिक स्वास्थ्य केंद्र पंजी — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre Kanpur Nagar]] ग्राम [[VILLAGE|Shivpuri]] जिला [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ahmad Khan]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Male]] | रोगिणा त्रि-सप्ताहं यावत् निरन्तरं कासः, मृदु-ज्वरः, च भार-क्षयः प्रस्तुताः।

रोगी अहमद खान, शिवपुरी ग्रामस्य 19-वर्षीयः पुरुषः, प्राथमिक स्वास्थ्य केंद्रं प्रति त्रि-सप्ताहं यावत् उत्पादक-कास-उत्पत्तिः, अनिरन्तरं मृदु-ज्वरः, मासे प्रायः 4 किलोग्राम भार-क्षयः इति परिदेवनं कृतवान्। रोगी कदाचित् रात्रौ स्वेदः, मृदु-श्रान्तिः च सूचयति। पूर्व-चिकित्सा-इतिहासः कदाचित् चिन्ता-सम्बद्ध-प्रसङ्गेषु महत्त्वपूर्णः अस्ति। परीक्षणे, जीवन-चिह्नानि: BP 120/80 mmHg, HR 88/min, RR 18/min, Temp 99.2°F, SpO2 96% कक्ष-वायौ। वक्षः-परीक्षणे दक्षिण-ऊर्ध्व-भागे श्वास-ध्वनिः न्यूनः अस्ति, कदाचित् रोंची-सह। AFB स्मीयर तथा GeneXpert परीक्षणाय श्लेष्मा-परीक्षणस्य आदेशः दत्तः। रोगी TB लक्षणानां विषये उपदिष्टः, लक्षणानां वृद्धौ तत्क्षणं प्रत्यागन्तुं सूचितः। रोगी-युवावस्थां तथा सम्भावित-सम्पर्क-जोखिम-सहित-नगर-निवासं दृष्ट्वा, वक्षः-X-ray अनुशंसितः। रोगी-मूल्याङ्कन-प्रबन्धनाय कानपुर-जिला-चिकित्सालयं प्रति प्रेषितः। सम्पर्क-विवरणम्: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]। अनुवर्ती-कार्याय [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Fatima Begum]] आशा-कार्यकर्त्री सूचिता। रोगी-विवरणानि [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]] तथा [[VOTER_ID|VK7890123]] सत्यापितानि। रोगी-[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] तन्त्रे पञ्जीकृतः अस्ति। यदि पूर्व-चिकित्सा-अभिलेखः अस्ति तर्हि आनेतुं रोगी-सूचितः। परीक्षण-परिणामानां कृते 3 दिनेषु अनुवर्ती-निर्धारणं कृतम्।
generated_text_en:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre Kanpur Nagar]] Village [[VILLAGE|Shivpuri]] District [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ahmad Khan]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Male]] | Patient presented with persistent cough for 3 weeks, low-grade fever, and weight loss

Patient Ahmad Khan, a 19-year-old male from Shivpuri village, presented to the PHC with complaints of productive cough for three weeks, intermittent low-grade fever, and approximately 4 kg weight loss over the past month. The patient reports occasional night sweats and mild fatigue. Past medical history is significant for occasional anxiety-related episodes. On examination, vitals: BP 120/80 mmHg, HR 88/min, RR 18/min, Temp 99.2°F, SpO2 96% on room air. Chest examination reveals decreased breath sounds in the right upper zone with occasional rhonchi. Sputum examination was ordered for AFB smear and GeneXpert testing. Patient was counseled about TB symptoms and advised to return immediately if symptoms worsen. Given the patient's young age and urban residence with potential exposure risks, a chest X-ray was recommended. The patient was referred to the District Hospital Kanpur for further evaluation and management. Contact details: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. ASHA worker [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Fatima Begum]] was informed for follow-up. Patient's [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]] and [[VOTER_ID|VK7890123]] details verified. Patient's [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] is registered in the system. Patient advised to bring previous medical records if any. Follow-up scheduled in 3 days for test results.
judge_reasoning: Sanskrit Devanagari prose with allowed Latin IDs/terms; TB content fits 19yo male persona; all TYPEs in allow-list; no length/script/domain violations.

professional_persona: Maino Murmu is a 32‑year‑old wireman who excels at installing and maintaining rural power systems, using a sharp eye for safety and spontaneous ‘jugaad’ improvisations to keep villages lit, while their competitive streak fuels a drive to earn a formal electrician certification and eventually lead a women‑run electrical cooperative.
linguistic_persona: Maino Murmu grew up speaking Santali at home and can converse fluently in it, manages basic Hindi interactions at work, picks up a handful of English terms during training, yet remains functionally illiterate in all languages, which pushes them to rely heavily on oral communication and memory.
sports_persona: Maino Murmu passionately follows the Jharkhand women's cricket team and the local Ranchi Royals in the regional league, regularly joins village women’s cricket matches, and they also enjoy playing Ludo and taking brisk walks in the nearby forest for fitness.
arts_persona: Maino Murmu delights in singing traditional folk songs and drumming on the dhodka, and they are also an avid fan of A.R. Rahman’s film scores, Shreya Ghoshal’s melodies, and the folk troupe “Munda Beats” that performs at village festivals.
travel_persona: Maino Murmu prefers short, budget‑friendly trips to nearby Hazaribagh Wildlife Sanctuary, the sacred Baidyanath hills, and occasional pilgrimages to Puri, and they always travel by bus with family, saving every spare rupee for the next journey.
culinary_persona: Maino Murmu enjoys the earthy flavors of litti chokha, the spicy fish curry (macha tarkari) with mustard seeds, relishes jaggery‑sweetened tea and fresh bamboo shoots, and they often treat themselves to mahua‑infused sweets like chhena poda during festivals.
persona: Maino Murmu is a spontaneous, competitive wireman who blends traditional folk spirit with modern electrical skills, yet they battle frequent anxiety and a deep love for community celebrations.
cultural_background: Maino belongs to the Santhal tribal community, one of the largest Adivasi groups in Jharkhand. Her family lives in a modest kachcha house in a village near Ranchi, where rice fields, forest patches and traditional wooden homes shape daily life. The Santhals celebrate Sohrai (harvest festival) and Baha (flower festival) with folk songs, drums and dance, reinforcing strong community bonds. While the tribe maintains its indigenous rituals and clan‑based social structure, an increasing number of members, including Maino, are engaging in modern occupations such as electrical work, reflecting a gradual ‘jugaad’ blend of tradition and new technology.
linguistic_background: Maino grew up speaking Santali at home and in her community. She never learned to read or write, so she is functionally illiterate. In her work as a wireman, she picks up technical terms that are spoken in Hindi and a few English words during on‑the‑job training. Her oral communication is strong in Santali, and she can manage basic conversations in Hindi when required, but she cannot read or write in any language.
skills_and_expertise: Maino has solid practical skills as a wireman in the light and power sector. She can install, maintain, and repair domestic and small‑scale agricultural electrical systems, including laying cables, connecting switches and sockets, and troubleshooting short circuits. Her on‑the‑job learning has given her a keen eye for safety, and she often improvises solutions using locally available materials—a hallmark of tribal ‘jugaad’. She is comfortable working in hot, humid rural conditions and can handle ladders and hand tools. Her strong oral communication helps her coordinate with fellow workers and explain safety steps to villagers. She remains cautious and double‑checks new equipment because of her innate skepticism, and she quickly adapts to unexpected changes thanks to her spontaneous nature.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Electrical wiring installation", "Circuit troubleshooting", "Safety compliance", "Improvisation using local resources (jugaad)", "Team coordination through oral communication", "Physical stamina for field work", "Basic voltage measurement knowledge", "Adaptability to spontaneous tasks"]
hobbies_and_interests: Maino enjoys singing traditional Santhal folk songs, dancing to the rhythm of the dhodka drum, and taking part in community festivals and wedding celebrations. In her spare time she likes to tinker with electrical gadgets and experiment with small repairs around the house. She often joins local women’s cricket matches or plays Ludo with neighbours, relishing the friendly competition. When feeling stressed, she finds calm in gardening and short walks through the nearby forest, appreciating nature’s tranquility.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Singing traditional Santhal folk songs", "Dancing to the dhodka drum", "Tinkering with electrical gadgets", "Participating in community festivals and weddings", "Playing cricket with local women", "Playing Ludo with neighbours", "Gardening", "Walking in the nearby forest"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Maino's career goal is to become a certified electrician and eventually lead a team that provides reliable power to villages across Jharkhand. She wants to acquire training in solar wiring and renewable energy installations so she can introduce low‑cost solar lighting to her community. Over the next five years, she hopes to start a women‑run electrical services cooperative, mentor other tribal girls interested in the trade, and earn enough to support her children's education. While tight deadlines sometimes trigger anxiety, she leverages her spontaneity as quick problem‑solving ability and uses her competitive nature to push herself through certification exams and on‑site challenges.
persona_clinical_summary: A 32-year-old female wireman from Ranchi, Jharkhand, currently married, with a rural occupation and no formal education. She is functionally illiterate, speaks Santali at home, and works installing and maintaining rural power systems. Clinically, she presents with frequent anxiety and uses Hindi and some English technical terms in her work.
system_prompt:

Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Santali (code=sat, script=Ol Chiki). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Ranchi.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Wireman, Light and Power
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Ranchi
- first_language: Santali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 32-year-old female wireman from Ranchi, Jharkhand, currently married, with a rural occupation and no formal education. She is functionally illiterate, speaks Santali at home, and works installing and maintaining rural power systems. Clinically, she presents with frequent anxiety and uses Hindi and some English technical terms in her work.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ᱢᱟᱨᱮ ᱨᱟᱱ ᱱᱟᱜᱟᱢ:

ᱵᱤᱱᱤᱰ:

ᱢᱩᱞᱭᱟᱭᱚᱱ:
᱑. Chronic fatigue syndrome ᱥᱟᱶᱛᱮ ᱦᱩᱭ ᱫᱟᱲᱮᱭᱟᱜ ᱠᱮᱱᱥᱟᱨ
᱒. ᱵᱟᱝ ᱵᱟᱰᱟᱭ ᱛᱮ ᱦᱟᱢᱟᱞ ᱠᱚᱢᱚᱜ

Chief complaint: Persistent fatigue and intermittent chest pain for 3 months with recent weight loss of 8 kg

History of present illness: Patient reports progressive fatigue over past 3 months, initially attributing to work-related physical exertion as a wireman. Three months ago developed intermittent substernal chest pain described as sharp, lasting 5-10 minutes, occurring 2-3 times weekly. Pain worsens with exertion and improves with rest. Associated with mild dyspnea on exertion. Reports unintentional weight loss of approximately 8 kg despite maintained appetite. Denies fever, night sweats, or hemoptysis.

Past medical history: No known chronic illnesses. No previous hospitalizations. No known allergies.

Physical examination:
- General: Thin, appears fatigued, cachectic
- Vital signs: BP 130/85 mmHg, Pulse 92/min, Temperature 36.8°C, SpO2 94% on room air
- Cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops

- Chest X-ray: Widened mediastinum with possible hilar enlargement
- ECG: Sinus rhythm, non-specific ST changes

Assessment:

5. Follow-up appointment in 1 week

Patient counseled regarding symptoms requiring immediate attention. Relative present and educated about disease process.

professional_persona: Maino Murmu is a 32‑year‑old wireman who excels at installing and maintaining rural power systems, using a sharp eye for safety and spontaneous ‘jugaad’ improvisations to keep villages lit, while their competitive streak fuels a drive to earn a formal electrician certification and eventually lead a women‑run electrical cooperative.
linguistic_persona: Maino Murmu grew up speaking Santali at home and can converse fluently in it, manages basic Hindi interactions at work, picks up a handful of English terms during training, yet remains functionally illiterate in all languages, which pushes them to rely heavily on oral communication and memory.
sports_persona: Maino Murmu passionately follows the Jharkhand women's cricket team and the local Ranchi Royals in the regional league, regularly joins village women’s cricket matches, and they also enjoy playing Ludo and taking brisk walks in the nearby forest for fitness.
arts_persona: Maino Murmu delights in singing traditional folk songs and drumming on the dhodka, and they are also an avid fan of A.R. Rahman’s film scores, Shreya Ghoshal’s melodies, and the folk troupe “Munda Beats” that performs at village festivals.
travel_persona: Maino Murmu prefers short, budget‑friendly trips to nearby Hazaribagh Wildlife Sanctuary, the sacred Baidyanath hills, and occasional pilgrimages to Puri, and they always travel by bus with family, saving every spare rupee for the next journey.
culinary_persona: Maino Murmu enjoys the earthy flavors of litti chokha, the spicy fish curry (macha tarkari) with mustard seeds, relishes jaggery‑sweetened tea and fresh bamboo shoots, and they often treat themselves to mahua‑infused sweets like chhena poda during festivals.
persona: Maino Murmu is a spontaneous, competitive wireman who blends traditional folk spirit with modern electrical skills, yet they battle frequent anxiety and a deep love for community celebrations.
cultural_background: Maino belongs to the Santhal tribal community, one of the largest Adivasi groups in Jharkhand. Her family lives in a modest kachcha house in a village near Ranchi, where rice fields, forest patches and traditional wooden homes shape daily life. The Santhals celebrate Sohrai (harvest festival) and Baha (flower festival) with folk songs, drums and dance, reinforcing strong community bonds. While the tribe maintains its indigenous rituals and clan‑based social structure, an increasing number of members, including Maino, are engaging in modern occupations such as electrical work, reflecting a gradual ‘jugaad’ blend of tradition and new technology.
linguistic_background: Maino grew up speaking Santali at home and in her community. She never learned to read or write, so she is functionally illiterate. In her work as a wireman, she picks up technical terms that are spoken in Hindi and a few English words during on‑the‑job training. Her oral communication is strong in Santali, and she can manage basic conversations in Hindi when required, but she cannot read or write in any language.
skills_and_expertise: Maino has solid practical skills as a wireman in the light and power sector. She can install, maintain, and repair domestic and small‑scale agricultural electrical systems, including laying cables, connecting switches and sockets, and troubleshooting short circuits. Her on‑the‑job learning has given her a keen eye for safety, and she often improvises solutions using locally available materials—a hallmark of tribal ‘jugaad’. She is comfortable working in hot, humid rural conditions and can handle ladders and hand tools. Her strong oral communication helps her coordinate with fellow workers and explain safety steps to villagers. She remains cautious and double‑checks new equipment because of her innate skepticism, and she quickly adapts to unexpected changes thanks to her spontaneous nature.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Electrical wiring installation", "Circuit troubleshooting", "Safety compliance", "Improvisation using local resources (jugaad)", "Team coordination through oral communication", "Physical stamina for field work", "Basic voltage measurement knowledge", "Adaptability to spontaneous tasks"]
hobbies_and_interests: Maino enjoys singing traditional Santhal folk songs, dancing to the rhythm of the dhodka drum, and taking part in community festivals and wedding celebrations. In her spare time she likes to tinker with electrical gadgets and experiment with small repairs around the house. She often joins local women’s cricket matches or plays Ludo with neighbours, relishing the friendly competition. When feeling stressed, she finds calm in gardening and short walks through the nearby forest, appreciating nature’s tranquility.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Singing traditional Santhal folk songs", "Dancing to the dhodka drum", "Tinkering with electrical gadgets", "Participating in community festivals and weddings", "Playing cricket with local women", "Playing Ludo with neighbours", "Gardening", "Walking in the nearby forest"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Maino's career goal is to become a certified electrician and eventually lead a team that provides reliable power to villages across Jharkhand. She wants to acquire training in solar wiring and renewable energy installations so she can introduce low‑cost solar lighting to her community. Over the next five years, she hopes to start a women‑run electrical services cooperative, mentor other tribal girls interested in the trade, and earn enough to support her children's education. While tight deadlines sometimes trigger anxiety, she leverages her spontaneity as quick problem‑solving ability and uses her competitive nature to push herself through certification exams and on‑site challenges.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

She works in rural power systems, using technical terms in Hindi and English, and is married. Her lifestyle includes community celebrations and following regional cricket.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

professional_persona: Maino Murmu is a 32‑year‑old wireman who excels at installing and maintaining rural power systems, using a sharp eye for safety and spontaneous ‘jugaad’ improvisations to keep villages lit, while their competitive streak fuels a drive to earn a formal electrician certification and eventually lead a women‑run electrical cooperative.
linguistic_persona: Maino Murmu grew up speaking Santali at home and can converse fluently in it, manages basic Hindi interactions at work, picks up a handful of English terms during training, yet remains functionally illiterate in all languages, which pushes them to rely heavily on oral communication and memory.
sports_persona: Maino Murmu passionately follows the Jharkhand women's cricket team and the local Ranchi Royals in the regional league, regularly joins village women’s cricket matches, and they also enjoy playing Ludo and taking brisk walks in the nearby forest for fitness.
arts_persona: Maino Murmu delights in singing traditional folk songs and drumming on the dhodka, and they are also an avid fan of A.R. Rahman’s film scores, Shreya Ghoshal’s melodies, and the folk troupe “Munda Beats” that performs at village festivals.
travel_persona: Maino Murmu prefers short, budget‑friendly trips to nearby Hazaribagh Wildlife Sanctuary, the sacred Baidyanath hills, and occasional pilgrimages to Puri, and they always travel by bus with family, saving every spare rupee for the next journey.
culinary_persona: Maino Murmu enjoys the earthy flavors of litti chokha, the spicy fish curry (macha tarkari) with mustard seeds, relishes jaggery‑sweetened tea and fresh bamboo shoots, and they often treat themselves to mahua‑infused sweets like chhena poda during festivals.
persona: Maino Murmu is a spontaneous, competitive wireman who blends traditional folk spirit with modern electrical skills, yet they battle frequent anxiety and a deep love for community celebrations.
cultural_background: Maino belongs to the Santhal tribal community, one of the largest Adivasi groups in Jharkhand. Her family lives in a modest kachcha house in a village near Ranchi, where rice fields, forest patches and traditional wooden homes shape daily life. The Santhals celebrate Sohrai (harvest festival) and Baha (flower festival) with folk songs, drums and dance, reinforcing strong community bonds. While the tribe maintains its indigenous rituals and clan‑based social structure, an increasing number of members, including Maino, are engaging in modern occupations such as electrical work, reflecting a gradual ‘jugaad’ blend of tradition and new technology.
linguistic_background: Maino grew up speaking Santali at home and in her community. She never learned to read or write, so she is functionally illiterate. In her work as a wireman, she picks up technical terms that are spoken in Hindi and a few English words during on‑the‑job training. Her oral communication is strong in Santali, and she can manage basic conversations in Hindi when required, but she cannot read or write in any language.
skills_and_expertise: Maino has solid practical skills as a wireman in the light and power sector. She can install, maintain, and repair domestic and small‑scale agricultural electrical systems, including laying cables, connecting switches and sockets, and troubleshooting short circuits. Her on‑the‑job learning has given her a keen eye for safety, and she often improvises solutions using locally available materials—a hallmark of tribal ‘jugaad’. She is comfortable working in hot, humid rural conditions and can handle ladders and hand tools. Her strong oral communication helps her coordinate with fellow workers and explain safety steps to villagers. She remains cautious and double‑checks new equipment because of her innate skepticism, and she quickly adapts to unexpected changes thanks to her spontaneous nature.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Electrical wiring installation", "Circuit troubleshooting", "Safety compliance", "Improvisation using local resources (jugaad)", "Team coordination through oral communication", "Physical stamina for field work", "Basic voltage measurement knowledge", "Adaptability to spontaneous tasks"]
hobbies_and_interests: Maino enjoys singing traditional Santhal folk songs, dancing to the rhythm of the dhodka drum, and taking part in community festivals and wedding celebrations. In her spare time she likes to tinker with electrical gadgets and experiment with small repairs around the house. She often joins local women’s cricket matches or plays Ludo with neighbours, relishing the friendly competition. When feeling stressed, she finds calm in gardening and short walks through the nearby forest, appreciating nature’s tranquility.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Singing traditional Santhal folk songs", "Dancing to the dhodka drum", "Tinkering with electrical gadgets", "Participating in community festivals and weddings", "Playing cricket with local women", "Playing Ludo with neighbours", "Gardening", "Walking in the nearby forest"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Maino's career goal is to become a certified electrician and eventually lead a team that provides reliable power to villages across Jharkhand. She wants to acquire training in solar wiring and renewable energy installations so she can introduce low‑cost solar lighting to her community. Over the next five years, she hopes to start a women‑run electrical services cooperative, mentor other tribal girls interested in the trade, and earn enough to support her children's education. While tight deadlines sometimes trigger anxiety, she leverages her spontaneity as quick problem‑solving ability and uses her competitive nature to push herself through certification exams and on‑site challenges.
persona_clinical_summary: A 32-year-old female wireman from Ranchi, Jharkhand, who is currently married and works in rural power systems. She has a high school education and is functionally illiterate, speaking Santali at home. Clinically, she presents with frequent anxiety and a competitive personality. She lives in a modest rural kachcha house and prefers short, budget-friendly trips.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Santali (code=sat, script=Ol Chiki). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Ranchi.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Wireman, Light and Power
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Ranchi
- first_language: Santali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 32-year-old female wireman from Ranchi, Jharkhand, who is currently married and works in rural power systems. She has a high school education and is functionally illiterate, speaking Santali at home. Clinically, she presents with frequent anxiety and a competitive personality. She lives in a modest rural kachcha house and prefers short, budget-friendly trips.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]

- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, a veteran mukadam of the salt‑pan fields, they combine three decades of hands‑on evaporation expertise with a practical curiosity for modest low‑cost improvements, quietly coordinating labour crews, brine flow and quality checks while preserving traditional methods.
linguistic_persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, fluent in his community's native language and conversational in Bengali, they manage daily interactions with workers and market traders using oral communication, while their exposure to Hindi remains limited to festival listening.
sports_persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, a quiet enthusiast of early‑morning walks along the saline fields, they follow the Bengal cricket team and the Kolkata Knight Riders on TV, and occasionally join a village kabaddi circle on Sundays, preferring low‑impact activity that fits their age.
arts_persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, an avid listener of traditional folk ballads and the soulful renditions of local bard Jatra Kumar, they also enjoy Bollywood classics by Lata Mangeshkar and the films of Amitabh Bachchan, often humming these melodies while working.
travel_persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, who prefers modest travel, they have visited Shantiniketan for a cultural fair, trekked to Darjeeling’s tea gardens during a summer break, and made a pilgrimage to Puri’s Jagannath Temple, planning future trips to the Sundarbans only if finances allow.
culinary_persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, who favors hearty meals like salted fish curry with rice, fermented panta bhat, and wild basil leaves gathered from the marshes, they enjoy dining at local dhabas where they can savor papad, roasted pumpkin, and the occasional sweet pitha, though they rarely cook at home.
persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, a 56‑year‑old salt‑pan elder, they blend quiet curiosity with steadfast tradition, worry over market swings, and find peace in solitary walks, folk songs, and modest savings for their family.
cultural_background: Sannyasi Ghosh belongs to the Santali Adivasi community of Birbhum district in West Bengal. He grew up in a rural tribal setting where traditional customs of the Santal people—such as oral storytelling, worship of nature spirits, and collective work during harvest and salt extraction—co‑exist with the larger Bengali agrarian culture of the region. His family has historically been involved in salt production, a trade that has shaped the local economy for generations.
linguistic_background: Sannyasi speaks Santali fluently as his mother tongue and uses it for daily communication within his community. He also understands and speaks Bengali, which helps him interact with neighboring farmers, market traders and local officials. Although he is illiterate, he can recognise basic Devanagari symbols on religious signs and can follow oral instructions for salt‑pan work. He has limited exposure to Hindi, mainly through festivals and village gatherings, but he relies on listening rather than reading.
skills_and_expertise: Over three decades, Sannyasi has developed practical expertise in managing salt‑pan operations as a mukadam. He coordinates labour crews, monitors the flow of brine, adjusts sun‑drying schedules, and ensures quality control of the final salt crystals. He possesses strong hands‑on knowledge of traditional evaporation techniques, basic trade negotiation with local merchants, and community dispute resolution. His oral storytelling skill also helps preserve tribal folklore among younger workers.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Salt pan supervision", "Traditional salt drying techniques", "Labour coordination", "Basic trade negotiation", "Water management", "Oral storytelling", "Community mediation"]
hobbies_and_interests: Sannyasi enjoys solitary walks along the salt pans at dawn, listening to the gentle rustle of reeds and distant village chants. He loves hearing traditional Santali folk songs and sometimes hums them while working. Gathering wild herbs for personal remedies, crafting simple bamboo tools, and meditating quietly under the shade of a banyan tree are other activities that help him unwind and stay grounded.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Morning walks along salt pans", "Listening to Santali folk songs", "Herb gathering", "Bamboo crafting", "Quiet meditation under banyan tree", "Humming while working"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: At this stage, Sannyasi aims to secure a steady income for his family while passing on his salt‑production knowledge to his sons and younger workers. He hopes to introduce modest, low‑cost improvements—such as better brine channels and protective shade nets—to increase yield without disrupting traditional practices. He also wishes to be recognized as a trusted elder who can mediate community issues and safeguard the cultural heritage of the Santali tribe in the region.
persona_clinical_summary: A 56-year-old male from Birbhum, West Bengal, currently married, works as a salt production mukadam with an illiterate education level. He resides in a rural zone and speaks Santali as his first language, with functional Bengali. His lifestyle includes traditional Santali customs, market-related stress, and modest savings for his family.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER
